lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: They have tied me to a stake; I cannot fly,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But, bear-like, I must fight the course. What’s he

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That was not born of woman? Such a one

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Am I to fear, or none.

lines.speaker: YOUNG SIWARD
lines.text: What is thy name?

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou’lt be afraid to hear it.

lines.speaker: YOUNG SIWARD
lines.text: No; though thou call’st thyself a hotter name

lines.speaker: YOUNG SIWARD
lines.text: Than any is in hell.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: My name’s Macbeth.

lines.speaker: YOUNG SIWARD
lines.text: The devil himself could not pronounce a title

lines.speaker: YOUNG SIWARD
lines.text: More hateful to mine ear.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: No, nor more fearful.

lines.speaker: YOUNG SIWARD
lines.text: Thou liest, abhorred tyrant; with my sword

lines.speaker: YOUNG SIWARD
lines.text: I’ll prove the lie thou speak’st. They fight and Young Siward is slain.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Thou wast born of woman

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: But swords I smile at, weapons laugh to scorn,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Brandish’d by man that’s of a woman born.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: That way the noise is. Tyrant, show thy face!

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: If thou be’st slain and with no stroke of mine,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: My wife and children’s ghosts will haunt me still.

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: I cannot strike at wretched kerns, whose arms

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Are hired to bear their staves: either thou, Macbeth,

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Or else my sword with an unbatter’d edge

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: I sheathe again undeeded. There thou shouldst be;

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: By this great clatter, one of greatest note

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: Seems bruited. Let me find him, fortune!

lines.speaker: MACDUFF
lines.text: And more I beg not.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: This way, my lord; the castle’s gently render’d:

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: The tyrant’s people on both sides do fight;

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: The noble thanes do bravely in the war;

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: The day almost itself professes yours,

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: And little is to do.

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: We have met with foes

lines.speaker: MALCOLM
lines.text: That strike beside us.

lines.speaker: SIWARD
lines.text: Enter, sir, the castle.

glosses.phrase: They have tied me to a stake
glosses.definition: Macbeth figuring himself as a bear tied to a stake — a reference to bear-baiting, a popular Elizabethan entertainment
like a tethered bear — forced to fight
Irish foot-soldiers; here, hired mercenaries Macbeth leads
Macduff's prayer — fortune lead him to Macbeth
the murdered family Macduff will avenge

annotations.title: 'They have tied me to a stake'
annotations.body:
Macbeth figures himself as a bear in a bear-baiting pit — tied to a stake, unable to flee, forced to fight the dogs. The reference is specific: bear-baiting was a Southwark spectacle staged alongside plays in Shakespeare's period, often in the same theaters. The audience knew the image literally. Macbeth has become the attraction at an entertainment.

The image is his last piece of self-knowledge. He is no longer the 'Bellona's bridegroom' of 1.2; he is the bear who cannot run. His 'charmed life' ('none of woman born') is about to be tested by Young Siward and then Macduff. The first test he passes, because Young Siward is in fact woman-born; the second will break him.
sources: Tiffany Stern, *Documents of Performance in Early Modern England* (2009)

trials.prompt: 'They have tied me to a stake' references:
options: Bear-baiting — a spectacle staged alongside plays in Shakespeare's theaters; Macbeth is the attraction
